segments.title: Mitsumasa Yonai’s quiet memorial
segments.summary: A walk between Mitsumasa Yonai’s burial place and statue becomes a reflection on how a reserved public figure is remembered.
segments.body: Clarke visits the cemetery area where Mitsumasa Yonai is buried, then walks to a nearby but secluded statue. He reads the understated setting as appropriate to Yonai’s character rather than as a grand national spectacle. Clarke notes that the Japanese tourist board and government describe Yonai as the leader who saved Japan from total destruction, which makes the quiet memorial especially striking. The video moment uses the memorial landscape to describe a figure who, in Clarke’s telling, worked through difficult problems quietly and often out of public view. It is a useful watch point for considering how commemoration can express an interpretation of a historical leader’s public style.

evidence.note: Clarke describes Yonai's burial place, statue, and preference for quiet problem-solving over public spectacle.

segments.title: Why Yonai matters in Clarke’s view of postwar Japan
segments.summary: Clarke explains why he wanted to see Yonai’s memorial and presents him as an important, often overlooked figure in Japan’s postwar story.
segments.body: Clarke says that sustained historical study can make past figures feel personally significant to a historian. That feeling leads him to seek reassurance that Yonai is remembered in a way Yonai himself might have accepted. He recommends the visit to viewers in Japan and argues that Yonai deserves greater recognition. The assessment is explicitly Clarke’s: he connects Yonai to Japan’s survival after the Second World War and to the treatment Japan received from the Allies.

evidence.note: Clarke reflects on historical attachment, recommends visiting Yonai, and states his assessment of Yonai's postwar importance.
